teachingPoint: Access control must be provisioned (MembershipActivated stored) before content retrieval starts. Branch B (T104 HistoricalContentSeeder) triggers on MembershipActivated, not on the initial join request. Triggering T104 on the join request means content is seeded for a user whose membership may be denied (private group pending approval), creating a data leakage window.
positiveExample: T104 @EventPattern('MembershipActivated') — only fires after T101 stores ACTIVE membership.
negativeExample: T104 @EventPattern('GroupJoinRequested') — fires immediately, seeds content before access is confirmed.
discriminatingConstraint: Branch B entry event is MembershipActivated, never GroupJoinRequested. The event name is the gate.
keywords: Access control provisioned MembershipActivated content retrieval starts Branch T104 HistoricalContentSeeder triggers initial

teachingPoint: Membership tier must be read from the subscription record (xiigen-subscriptions), never from the join request payload. T100 queries xiigen-subscriptions by userId to resolve tier. Client-submitted tier in the join request is ignored. A client submitting tier='PREMIUM' on a FREE subscription would silently escalate their access tier.
positiveExample: T100: db.searchDocuments('xiigen-subscriptions', {userId}) → resolves tier from stored subscription.
negativeExample: T100: return { tier: input.membershipTier } — client controls their own access tier.
discriminatingConstraint: membershipTier field does not exist in T100's input shape. Tier comes only from xiigen-subscriptions.
keywords: Membership tier read subscription xiigen-subscriptions join request payload T100 queries userId resolve

teachingPoint: Private group joins create a PENDING membership record, not an ACTIVE one. T102 reads group.type from the group record: PUBLIC → status='ACTIVE', PRIVATE → status='PENDING'. Writing ACTIVE for a private group bypasses the approval workflow — the member gains immediate access without admin review.
positiveExample: T102: group.type === 'PRIVATE' ? { status: 'PENDING' } : { status: 'ACTIVE' }. MACHINE constant.
negativeExample: T102: { status: 'ACTIVE' } always — private group members gain immediate access, no approval flow.
discriminatingConstraint: status is derived from group.type — never from any input field. The derivation is MACHINE logic.
keywords: Private group joins create PENDING membership ACTIVE T102 reads type PUBLIC status

teachingPoint: Concurrent duplicate join prevention uses SETNX on (userId, groupId). T99 attempts to create a new membership record only if none exists. If a record already exists (from a concurrent request), the existing record is returned as a success — no error, no duplicate. This is idempotent: the second request returns the same outcome as the first.
positiveExample: T99: setnxDocument('xiigen-memberships', {userId, groupId}) → existing: return DataProcessResult.success(existing).
negativeExample: T99: searchDocuments → count > 0 → return failure('ALREADY_MEMBER') — second request returns error, not idempotent.
discriminatingConstraint: SETNX semantics: existing record = success (idempotent), not failure. Returning failure on duplicate breaks client retry logic.
keywords: Concurrent duplicate join prevention uses SETNX userId groupId attempts create membership none

teachingPoint: Tier change operations (T116/T117) use conditional update on the existing membership record, not SETNX. SETNX creates a new record only if none exists — it would silently no-op on an existing membership, leaving the tier unchanged. Tier changes require modifying an existing record's tier field via updateDocument with a condition.
positiveExample: T116: db.updateDocument('xiigen-memberships', {userId, groupId}, {tier: newTier}) — update existing.
negativeExample: T116: db.setnxDocument('xiigen-memberships', {userId, groupId, tier: newTier}) — no-op if record exists, tier never changes.
discriminatingConstraint: SETNX vs conditional update is determined by operation type: new-record idempotency vs existing-record mutation.
keywords: Tier change operations T116 T117 conditional update existing membership SETNX none exists

teachingPoint: Historical content seeding must always pass the resolved membershipTier to the content query. T105 retrieves historical content from xiigen-group-content filtered by tier <= memberTier. Without the tier filter, a FREE tier member would see PREMIUM-only content during their initial feed population — a silent access violation.
positiveExample: T105: searchDocuments('xiigen-group-content', {groupId, tier: {lte: memberTier}}) — tier filter always applied.
negativeExample: T105: searchDocuments('xiigen-group-content', {groupId}) — all content regardless of tier. Free members see premium content.
discriminatingConstraint: membershipTier is a required parameter to the content query. Absence = access control bypass.
keywords: Historical content seeding pass resolved membershipTier query T105 retrieves xiigen-group-content filtered tier

teachingPoint: The approval gate chain (SUBFLOW-06P) requires strict ordering: T112 sends approval notification → T114 stores AccessGranted record → T115 stores ACTIVE membership (DNA-8). Reversing T114 and T115 (storing ACTIVE membership before AccessGranted) violates the audit trail: the access grant event is missing at the time the membership is ACTIVE.
positiveExample: T112 notify → T114 storeDocument(AccessGranted) → T115 storeDocument(status=ACTIVE) → emit MembershipActivated.
negativeExample: T112 → T115 storeDocument(ACTIVE) → T114 storeDocument(AccessGranted) — audit record created after access granted.
discriminatingConstraint: AccessGranted record is the audit evidence. It must exist BEFORE ACTIVE status. DNA-8 applies within subflows too.
keywords: approval gate chain SUBFLOW-06P strict ordering T112 sends notification T114 AccessGranted T115
Content retrieval branches trigger access-confirmation events MembershipActivated join-request GroupJoinRequested Triggering seeding join
teachingPoint: Content retrieval branches must trigger on access-confirmation events (MembershipActivated), not on join-request events (GroupJoinRequested). Triggering content seeding on the join request creates a data leakage window for private groups: content is available before access is confirmed. CF-06-1 enforces this ordering.
ironRules: Content seeding starts ONLY after access is confirmed — not on join request
CF-06-1: any content branch triggering on GroupJoinRequested is a BUILD_FAILURE
codeSnippet:
@EventPattern('MembershipActivated') // CORRECT: access confirmed
// Not: @EventPattern('GroupJoinRequested') // access not yet confirmed — private group leakage risk

keywords: Membership tier resolution server-side T100 queries xiigen-subscriptions userId active subscription derives join
teachingPoint: Membership tier resolution is server-side only. T100 queries xiigen-subscriptions for the userId's active subscription and derives tier from that record. The join request payload has no tier field. This mirrors the FLOW-05 SERVER-SIDE-CALCULATION pattern: in both cases, client-submitted privilege claims are structurally excluded.
ironRules: membershipTier field does not exist in T100 input shape — client cannot submit tier
T100 reads membershipTier from xiigen-subscriptions only — never from request input
Client-submitted tier = silent privilege escalation — CF-06-2

keywords: Group membership idempotency uses SETNX semantics userId groupId composite duplicate join attempt
teachingPoint: Group membership idempotency uses SETNX semantics: (userId, groupId) is the composite key. A duplicate join attempt returns the existing membership record as DataProcessResult.success — not an error. This matches FLOW-04's RSVP idempotency pattern. The difference: FLOW-06 SETNX applies to new-record creation only; tier changes use conditional update (DR-06-E).
ironRules: SETNX on (userId, groupId) — existing record returns DataProcessResult.success (idempotent)
Duplicate join attempt = success, not ALREADY_MEMBER error
SETNX applies to NEW record creation only — tier changes use updateDocument (DR-06-E)

keywords: content query tiered-access system include membership tier filter parameter optional omission access
teachingPoint: Any content query in a tiered-access system must include the membership tier as a filter parameter. The tier filter is not optional: omission is an access control bypass. This applies to historical seeding (T105) and ongoing feed composition (T106). CF-06-1 fires on any content query missing a tier predicate.
ironRules: Every content query includes tier filter: searchDocuments('xiigen-group-content', { groupId, tier: { lte: memberTier } })
Tier filter is mandatory — omission is a silent access control bypass
CF-06-1: any content query missing tier predicate is a BUILD_FAILURE
